1: veAta haqrev eleycha et-Aharon achicha: 'Bring near to yourself Aaron your brother.' The verb qarav (to bring near, approach) is the primary term for priestly access to YHWH — the priest is the one who is permitted to approach what is otherwise unapproachable. The appointment of Aaron and his four sons (Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar) from among the Israelites establishes the Aaronic priesthood as a subset of the people, appointed by divine sovereign choice — not hereditary nobility but divine calling. Hebrews 5:4 applies this principle to Christ: 'No one takes this honor for himself, but only when called by God, just as Aaron was.'
2: bigdei-kodesh (bigdê-qōdesh): 'sacred/holy garments' — dedicated garments that belong to the sphere of holiness (qodesh). The purpose stated: lecavod ul-tifaret ('for glory and for splendor/beauty') — the same pair used of YHWH's own nature in various doxologies. The high priest's vestments embody divine glory in visible, wearable form. The priest wearing bigdei kavod is clothed with the glory of the one he represents. Paul's 'clothe yourselves with Christ' (Galatians 3:27; Romans 13:14) draws on the vestment-as-identity metaphor: believers wear Christ as the priest wore the sacred garments.
3: chakhmei-lev (ḥakhmê-lēb): 'wise of heart' — the craftsmen appointed to make the priestly garments are those to whom YHWH has given ruach chakhmah (spirit of wisdom, Exodus 28:3). The craftsmanship of the sacred garments requires divine wisdom, not merely technical skill. The same divine wisdom-giving appears in Bezalel's commissioning (Exodus 31:3: 'I have filled him with the Spirit of God in wisdom'). The connection between the Spirit's presence and skillful sacred work is consistent throughout Exodus: the tabernacle is built under divine wisdom, not human ingenuity.
4: choshen ve-ephod ume'il vekhutonet tashbetz umitznefet ve'avnet: the six elements of the high priestly vestments — (1) choshen mishpat (breastpiece of judgment/decision); (2) ephod (the sacred apron/vestment); (3) me'il (the robe of the ephod, entirely blue); (4) kutonet tashbetz (woven/checkered tunic); (5) mitznefet (turban); (6) avnet (sash/girdle). Together these constitute the bigdei hakodesh — the full vestments that mark the high priest as the mediator between YHWH and Israel. Each element has both functional and symbolic significance, developed in the chapters following. Hebrews sees the entire Aaronic high-priestly role as a type of Christ's permanent and perfect high priesthood (Heb 7-10).
5: zahav utekhelet veargaman vetola'at shani veshesh: 'gold, blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen' — the five materials of the priestly vestments, identical to the five materials of the tabernacle curtains (Exodus 26:1). The color sequence is consistently ordered: blue (tekhelet), purple (argaman), scarlet (tola'at shani). Tekhelet is the blue from the Murex snail, used also for the tassels (tzitzit, Numbers 15:38) as a reminder of the commandments. Purple was the most expensive dye in the ANE — associated with royalty. The high priestly colors encode royal and priestly identity: the high priest is simultaneously priest and (symbolic) king, prefiguring the Melchizedekian pattern (Genesis 14:18; Psalms 110:4; Heb 7).
6: ephod (ʾēp̄ōd, H646): the sacred apron/vestment, the defining garment of priestly identity. The word's etymology is uncertain; the ephod appears in various contexts as both a priestly garment (here) and an oracular object (Judges 8:27; Exodus 17:5). The high priestly ephod is made of the finest materials including woven gold thread — an extraordinary luxury item. The ephod bears the choshen (breastpiece) and the avnei shoham (onyx stones) with the tribal names. David wearing a linen ephod before the ark (2 Samuel 6:14) echoes the high priestly role — the Davidic king functioning in priestly capacity, anticipating the Melchizedekian priest-king of Ps 110.
7: shtei ktafot chovrot: 'two shoulder pieces joining it together' — the ephod is joined by two shoulder pieces (ktafot, shoulders). The shoulders in biblical imagery carry weight and responsibility: the government is 'on his shoulder' (Isaiah 9:6, of the Messiah); the lost sheep is carried on the shepherd's shoulders (Luke 15:5). The high priest's shoulders bear the names of Israel (Exodus 28:12) — he carries the whole nation on his shoulders before YHWH. Christ's high-priestly bearing of his people is visualized in this architectural detail.
8: cheshev epudato: 'the skilled waistband of the ephod' — the cheshev (artistic band/waistband) is woven from the same materials as the ephod and forms a single piece with it. The term cheshev (H2805) is from chashav (to think, plan, design) — the band is the work of skilled designing (machashevet). The unity of the waistband with the ephod (they are one piece) pictures the integrity of the priestly vestment: all elements form a coherent whole, nothing attached carelessly.
9: avnei shoham (onyx stones, H7718): two onyx stones engraved with the names of the twelve sons of Israel (six on each stone). The onyx is described as being set in mishbetzot zahav (gold filigree settings). The tribal names engraved on precious stones are a permanent inscription — the names are cut in, not painted over, and the stones are valuable. The priest carries Israel's identity in permanent, costly form on his shoulders. Compare Revelation 21:12-14 where the twelve gates and twelve foundations of the new Jerusalem bear the names of the twelve tribes and twelve apostles — the priestly bearing of Israel's names finds its eschatological fulfillment in the new Jerusalem itself.
10: betoledotam: 'in the order of their birth' — the six names on each stone are ordered by birth sequence, not by tribal prominence or later canonical order. The historical order of birth (Reuben through Joseph and Benjamin) establishes the continuity between the patriarchal narratives and the tabernacle priesthood: the same twelve sons of Jacob whose story fills Genesis are now permanently inscribed on the high priest's garments. The priest carries not only the present community but the entire history of the covenant line.
11: pituchei chotam: 'engravings of a seal/signet' — the names are engraved on the stones the way a professional seal-engraver cuts a signet ring (chotam, H2368). The signet ring in the ANE was a person's legal identity — to press one's signet into clay was to commit oneself legally. The high priest bears Israel's names engraved as legal seals on his shoulders: he is the legal representative, the advocate (parakletos, cf. 1 John 2:1), bearing the people's identity before the divine court. Each stone is set in gold (mishbetzot zahav), the most honorable setting available.
12: avnei zikaron livenei Yisrael veNasa Aharon et-shemotam lifnei YHWH al-shtei ktefav lezikaron: 'stones of remembrance for the sons of Israel; Aaron shall bear their names before YHWH on his two shoulders as a memorial.' The threefold repetition — bear their names, before YHWH, memorial — establishes the theology: the high priest's primary function is representational intercession. He brings Israel's names into the divine presence. Zikaron (memorial) — YHWH “remembers” his covenant commitments; the memorial stones activate covenant memory. This is precisely what Christ does as our great high priest: he carries us before the Father permanently (Hebrews 7:25: 'he always lives to make intercession for them').
13: mishbetzot zahav: gold filigree settings — the most technically refined goldsmithing, using twisted wire meshwork to hold precious stones. The word mishbetzot (H4865) suggests a latticed or checkered cage of gold wire. The settings connect the breastpiece chains to the shoulder pieces of the ephod. The entire construction uses gold as the primary connecting material — the most precious and imperishable metal — linking the people's names (on the shoulder stones) to the decision-making breastpiece at the heart.
14: sharsherot gabiot: 'twisted chains, rope-like' — pure gold chains braided like rope. The imagery of twisted gold chains connecting the breastpiece to the shoulder stones pictures the inseparable link between the priest's bearing of the people's names (on shoulders) and his intercession for their justice/decisions (at the heart). The chains are elaborately constructed (gabiot, twisted/braided) to be both beautiful and strong — the priestly intercession is both aesthetically glorious and structurally unbreakable.
15: choshen mishpat (ḥōšen mišpāṭ): 'breastpiece of judgment/decision' — worn over Aaron's heart (al-libo, Exodus 28:29-30). Mishpat (H4941) covers the semantic range of judgment, justice, legal decision, and ordinance. The breastpiece is the medium through which YHWH renders decisions for Israel: the Urim and Thummim (Exodus 28:30) are placed within it. The physical location — over the heart — is theologically significant: YHWH's decisions for Israel are rendered through the priest's heart, the seat of will and affection. The priest is not a detached functionary but a person whose heart is fully engaged with Israel's fate before YHWH.
16: zeret orko vezeret rochbo: 'a span long and a span wide' — the breastpiece is square, one zeret (span, H2239 — the distance from the thumb to the little finger when spread, approximately 9 inches) in each dimension, and folded double to form a pocket. The doubling creates a pouch that holds the Urim and Thummim (Exodus 28:30). The square form is significant: the holy of holies is cubic (1 Kings 6:20), the new Jerusalem is a cube (Revelation 21:16), the altar's top is square (Exodus 27:1) — squareness represents divine completeness and order.
17: ve'milata vo miluat even arba turim even: 'you shall fill it with stone settings, four rows of stones' — the breastpiece holds twelve stones in four rows of three, one for each tribe of Israel. The stones (even, H68) are precious gems. The first row: odem (carnelian/ruby), pitda (chrysolite/topaz), bareqet (emerald/malachite). The identification of the specific stones is uncertain as ancient gem nomenclature does not map precisely to modern terminology. What is certain is that twelve distinct precious stones, each unique, represent the twelve distinct tribes — diverse but equally valued before YHWH.
18: veHashor sheni nofech sapir veyahalom: 'the second row: turquoise, lapis lazuli, and moonstone/diamond.' Sapir (H5601) is typically translated sapphire or lapis lazuli — the deep blue stone associated with the divine throne (Exodus 24:10: under YHWH's feet was like a pavement of sapir; Ezekiel 1:26: the throne-appearance like sapir). The presence of the sapphire/lapis lazuli in the breastpiece connects the priestly judgment-stones to the divine throne-imagery — the priest who wears the breastpiece stands before the same throne that sapphire/lapis underlies.
19: veHashor hashlishi leshem shevo ve'achlamah: 'the third row: jacinth/ligure, agate, and amethyst.' The stones of the breastpiece cover the full spectrum of precious gems known to the ancient world — red, blue, green, yellow, purple, white. The full spectrum encodes a theology: Israel's twelve tribes represent the full range of human diversity, all equally precious to YHWH, all equally requiring the priest's intercession. The twelve-stone breastpiece is the OT's most visual statement of YHWH's equal regard for the whole covenant community regardless of tribal rank.
20: veHashor harevi'i tarshish veshaoham veyashfeh meshubatzim zahav yihyu bemillu'otam: 'the fourth row: chrysolite, onyx, and jasper, set in gold filigree in their mounting.' Tarshish (H8658) is uncertain but may be beryl or chrysolite — associated with Tarshish in Spain or with the gleaming wheels of the divine chariot (Ezekiel 1:16: 'the appearance of the wheels was like tarshish'). Yashfeh (jasper) appears as the first stone of the new Jerusalem's foundation wall (Revelation 21:19), and the appearance of the one on the throne is like jasper (Revelation 4:3) — the high priestly breastpiece stones are distributed into the eschatological new Jerusalem's foundational structure.
21: veha'avanim tihyena al-shemot benei Yisrael: 'the stones shall be for the names of the sons of Israel' — explicitly: twelve stones, twelve names, twelve tribes. The one-to-one correspondence between tribe and gem is stated clearly, though the specific tribal assignments for each gem are not given here (a later tradition in Rev 21 assigns stones to the apostolic foundations). The breastpiece as a whole functions as a comprehensive intercession: the priest prays for the whole nation, not for favorites. Hebrews 7:25's 'he always lives to make intercession' has this all-comprehensive priestly inclusiveness in view.
22: veasita al-hachoshen sharshrot gabiot ma'aseh avot zahav tahor: 'for the breastpiece make twisted chains of pure gold, rope-work.' The breastpiece is attached to the ephod by matching chains and rings — the connections are multiple and reinforced. The entire construction of rings, chains, filigree, and cords creates a garment that cannot be accidentally displaced: the breastpiece is fixed over the heart by design. The permanence of the priest's intercession is encoded in the structural stability of the vestment: Aaron cannot minister before YHWH with the breastpiece separated from the ephod (Exodus 28:28).
23: vetasita al-hachoshen shtei taba'ot zahav: 'make two gold rings for the breastpiece.' The gold rings at the corners of the breastpiece form the attachment points for the chains. The doubling of rings (two at the top corners, two at the bottom corners) and the doubled cord (blue cord at the bottom, gold chains at the top) reflects the meticulous security of the construction. The high priestly vestment system is engineered so that nothing can fall off or come loose during the ritual service.
24: venatata et-shtei anefot hasharsherot: 'attach the two chains of gold to the two rings.' The chains connect the top corners of the breastpiece to the gold filigree settings on the shoulder pieces. The geometry of the connection ensures that when Aaron stands upright in the holy place, the breastpiece hangs naturally over his heart. The engineering of the vestment is inseparable from its theology: the breastpiece must lie over the heart, because the priest's intercession for Israel is a matter of the heart, not merely of duty.
25: vet-shtei qetzot shtei ha'anefot titen al-shtei hammishbetzot: 'the other ends of the chains attach to the two settings' on the shoulder pieces. The breastpiece-to-shoulder connection creates a structural integrity: the bearing of names on the shoulders (Exodus 28:9-12) is mechanically linked to the bearing of names on the heart (Exodus 28:29). The same priestly act that lifts Israel's names on the shoulders of intercession also places those names over the heart of compassion: power and affection work together in priestly intercession.
26: vetasita shtei taba'ot zahav vesamta otam al-shtei qetzot hachoshen al-sfato: 'make two more gold rings and attach them to the two lower corners of the breastpiece on its inner edge.' The lower rings and the blue cord (Exodus 28:28) that connects the breastpiece to the ephod's waistband prevent the breastpiece from swinging outward. Multiple attachment points ensure stability. The combination of upper-chain and lower-cord attachments mirrors how effective intercession is held in place by both the dignity of the priest's authority (chains/shoulder) and the obedience of the priestly covenant (tekhelet/blue cord symbolizing commandment-keeping, Numbers 15:38).
27: vetasita shtei taba'ot zahav venatatah otam al-shtei ktafot ha'ephod: 'make two more gold rings and attach them to the bottom of the shoulder pieces on the front of the ephod, close to the seam above the ephod's waistband.' The precision of the lower attachment mirrors the precision of the upper: every connection is specified, every angle determined. The meticulous instructions for the vestments reflect the same principle as the meticulous tabernacle pattern: the earthly copy must match the heavenly original exactly (Exodus 25:9). Careless reproduction produces a lesser mediator; the copy must be faithful to the original.
28: veyirtzu et-hachoshen mitaba'otav el-taba'ot ha'ephod bifitil tekhelet: 'the breastpiece shall be bound to the rings of the ephod with a blue cord.' The tekhelet (blue cord) that fastens the breastpiece to the ephod is the same blue used throughout the tabernacle system. Blue (tekhelet) in Israel's symbolism is the color of heaven and of the commandments (Numbers 15:38-40: blue cord on tassels to remind Israel of YHWH's commandments). The breastpiece is fastened to the ephod not by gold alone but by the blue of commandment-obedience: the priestly office is inseparable from Torah-faithful living. The cord specifically prevents the breastpiece from separating from the ephod — their unity is maintained by the blue of covenant fidelity.
29: veNasa Aharon et-shemot benei Yisrael bachoshen hamishpat al-libo bevaoo el-hakodesh lezikaron lifnei YHWH tamid: 'Aaron shall bear the names of the sons of Israel on the breastpiece of judgment over his heart when he goes into the holy place, as a continual memorial before YHWH.' Three key terms: (1) al-libo ('over his heart') — not on his shoulders only (Exodus 28:12) but over the heart, the seat of love and compassion; (2) lezikaron ('as a memorial') — activating YHWH's covenant memory; (3) tamid ('continually, always') — the intercession is perpetual, not occasional. Hebrews 7:25 applies all three: Christ as high priest 'always (pantote) lives to make intercession for them,' carrying their names before the Father perpetually and with the full engagement of his love.
30: Urim ve-Thummim (ûrîm wĕtummîm): 'Lights and Perfections' or possibly 'Curses and Completeness' — the translucent oracular stones placed in the fold of the breastpiece and used to determine YHWH's will in specific decisions (Numbers 27:21; 1 Samuel 28:6). The exact mechanism is unknown: they may have been two lots that gave binary yes/no answers, or their luminosity may have indicated YHWH's response. What is clear: the Urim and Thummim were not divination tools (forbidden in Deuteronomy) but divinely authorized means of inquiry. Thummim is from the same root as tamim (perfect, without blemish) — 'Perfection.' In Christ, the Urim and Thummim find their fulfillment: he is both the Light (John 8:12) and the Perfection (Hebrews 7:28) who communicates the Father's will directly and completely.
31: me'il ha'ephod: 'the robe of the ephod' — a long robe worn under the ephod, made entirely of blue (kulo tekhelet). The robe of the ephod is the second layer of the high priestly vestment, beneath the ephod and above the tunic. Its solid blue color encodes the priestly calling to full alignment with heaven's order — not partially colored, not decorative, but entirely the blue of the commandments. The bell-and-pomegranate hem (Exodus 28:33-35) adds acoustic and symbolic dimensions to the garment.
32: peh rosho betecho safah yihyeh lefiv saviv: 'there shall be a woven opening for the head, with a woven collar around the opening.' The reinforced collar (safah, binding/hem) prevents the robe from tearing when put on and taken off. The robe must not be torn: wearing a torn robe was a sign of mourning or disgrace. The Talmud (Yoma 72a) notes that a torn high priestly garment disqualifies the priest from service. The robe's integrity is essential — a torn robe means a compromised mediation. When the high priest Caiaphas tore his robes (Matthew 26:65) at Jesus's trial, he was performing the very act that signaled the end of his legitimate high-priestly function — the true high priest was standing before him.
33: rimonim (pomegranates, H7416): along the hem of the robe, alternating with gold bells — pomegranates of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn. The pomegranate (rimon) is one of the seven species of the promised land (Deuteronomy 8:8) and appears throughout the tabernacle and temple symbolism (1 Kings 7:18-20: 200 pomegranates on the temple pillars). Its many seeds make it a symbol of abundance and fruitfulness. The alternating bells and pomegranates (Exodus 28:33-34) create a rhythmic liturgical pattern: sound-symbol, life-symbol, sound-symbol, life-symbol — the priestly ministry is simultaneously heard (bells) and fruitful (pomegranates).
34: paamon zahav verimmon paamon zahav verimmon al-shole hame'il saviv: 'a gold bell and a pomegranate, alternating, around the hem of the robe.' The alternating pattern is explicit: bell, pomegranate, bell, pomegranate — equal numbers, regular spacing. The complete regularity of the pattern mirrors the complete regularity of the priestly ministry: YHWH's service admits no improvisation, no careless approximation. The bells announce the priest; the pomegranates represent the fruit of the priestly ministry. The priest announces YHWH's presence (bells as proclamation) and produces life in the community (pomegranates as abundance).
35: veHayah al-Aharon lesharet venishma qolo bevo'o el-hakodesh lifnei YHWH uvetzeto velo yamut: 'Aaron shall wear it when he ministers; its sound shall be heard when he enters the holy place before YHWH, and when he comes out, so that he may not die.' The bells serve a protective liturgical function: the sound announces the high priest's movement and signals that a live, consecrated minister is present. The solemn warning velo yamut ('so that he shall not die') recurs in the context of approaching the holy (Leviticus 16:2, 13). Hebrews 10:19-22 draws out the contrast: Christ has opened the way into the holy place by his blood, so that we may 'draw near with confidence,' no longer under the threat of death that the bell-system protected against.
36: tzitz zahav tahor upatachta alav pituchei chotam KODESH L'YHWH: 'a pure gold plate, engrave on it like a seal engraving: HOLY TO YHWH.' The tzitz (H6731, flower/plate) is a pure gold frontlet worn on the high priest's turban (Exodus 28:37-38). The inscription qodesh l'YHWH (HOLY TO YHWH) is the theological summary of the entire high priestly ministry: the priest's highest identity is consecration to YHWH, set apart for his service exclusively. The inscription mirrors the first commandment's exclusive claim (Exodus 20:3: 'no other gods before me'). Christ is himself qodesh l'YHWH — 'the Holy One of God' (Mark 1:24; John 6:69) — and his people are made holy in him (1 Corinthians 1:30: 'Christ Jesus ... became for us ... sanctification').
37: unatata oto al-ptil tekhelet vehayah al-hamitznafet el-mul penei hamitznefet yihyeh: 'attach it by a blue cord to the turban; it is to be on the front of the turban.' The gold inscription-plate is fastened to the front of the turban by a blue cord — it faces forward, always visible when the priest faces YHWH. The forehead position (Exodus 28:38) is significant: the mezuzah's totafot (frontlets) of Deuteronomy 6:8 are worn on the forehead as a constant reminder of YHWH's word. The priestly forehead-inscription makes the priest himself a living mezuzah: his identity proclaims YHWH's holiness wherever he goes.
38: veNasa Aharon et-avon hakodashim asher yaqdeshu benei Yisrael lechol-matanot qodsheihem: 'Aaron shall bear the guilt arising from the holy gifts that the Israelites consecrate for all their holy gifts.' The high priest's function is not only to carry Israel's names before YHWH (Exodus 28:12, 29) but to nasa avon (bear the iniquity/guilt) of the people's imperfect offerings. Even the holiest acts of Israel's worship are contaminated by human sin; the high priest bears that contamination. Lifnei YHWH tamid ('before YHWH continually') — the guilt-bearing is also perpetual. This is the verse that most directly anticipates Christ's high-priestly guilt-bearing: 'he himself bore our sins in his body' (1 Peter 2:24), permanently and completely, so that our imperfect worship is accepted through him.
39: veshivatzta hakutonet shesh: 'weave the tunic of fine linen' — the innermost layer of the high priest's vestments. Shesh (fine white linen, H8336 — possibly Egyptian linen) is the purest, whitest fabric available. White linen throughout the tabernacle system represents purity and righteousness (Revelation 19:8: 'the fine linen is the righteous deeds of the saints'). The turban (mitznefet) and sash (avnet) complete the high priest's ensemble. The layered vestments — linen tunic, blue robe, woven ephod, gold-adorned breastpiece, gold frontlet — build from inner purity outward to public glory, from private devotion to public representation.
40: ulevnei Aharon taase kutnot: 'for Aaron's sons make tunics' — the ordinary priests also receive sacred garments, though simpler than the high priest's. The same principle applies: lecavod ul-tifaret ('for glory and splendor'), reflecting the dignity of priestly service at any level. The democratization of priestly dignity (all priests, not only the high priest, receive vestments of glory) anticipates the NT's royal priesthood: all believers are priests (1 Peter 2:9) and all are clothed with Christ's righteousness (Galatians 3:27).
41: umashacha otam umileta et-yadam vekidashta otam: 'anoint them, ordain them (fill their hands), and consecrate them.' Three actions constitute priestly commissioning: (1) meshachta (māšaḥtā, anoint) — pour oil on the head; the root of mashiach (anointed one, Messiah). (2) mileta et-yadam ('fill their hands') — the standard OT idiom for ordination; the hands filled with the portions of the offering symbolize the priest's right and responsibility to handle the sacred things. (3) kidashta otam (consecrate/sanctify them) — set them apart as holy. All three are applied to Christ: he is the Messiah (anointed, Acts 10:38), his hands are filled with permanent priestly work (Hebrews 7:24-25), and he is set apart as holy (Hebrews 7:26).
42: michnesai-bad lekhassot besar-ervah: 'linen undergarments to cover the nakedness of the flesh' — the priestly undergarments (michnesayim, trousers/shorts) protect modesty when the priest ascends the altar steps. The explicit concern is that no nakedness (ervah) be exposed in YHWH's presence. The priestly garments cover what must not be seen before God — they envelop the priest in holiness from head to toe. The theme of covering (Genesis 3:21: YHWH clothed Adam and Eve) runs from Eden through the priesthood to Christ: he is himself the ultimate garment of righteousness that covers human nakedness before God.
43: vehayu al-Aharon veal-banav bevo'am el-ohel mo'ed o begishtam el-hamizbeach lesharet bakodesh velo yis'u avon umetu chuqat olam lo ulezar'o acharav: 'they shall be worn by Aaron and his sons when they enter the tent of meeting or approach the altar to minister in the holy place, so they will not incur guilt and die — a lasting ordinance for Aaron and his descendants.' The chapter closes with the warning that proper vestment is not optional: unrobed service in the holy place results in guilt and death. The entire system of vestments is a protective structure mediating between the holy God and the inadequate priest. Christ alone enters the divine presence without a protective covering because his own nature is the holiness the vestments symbolized — he is the High Priest who is 'holy, innocent, unstained' (Hebrews 7:26).
